perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California without regard to the conflict of laws provisions and shall be subject to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be subject to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California without regard to the conflict of laws provisions' could lead to the contract being interpreted under different laws, potentially disadvantaging one party, especially consumers who rely on California's consumer protection laws. This is particularly impactful regarding choice of law clauses in consumer contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the 'construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California' clause, the agreement opens the door to interpretation under laws outside of California, which might not provide the same level of consumer protection as California law. This could violate California's consumer protection laws, like the Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA), as it undermines the consumer's right to the protections afforded by California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person d
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause specifying that the agreement be construed in accordance with California law directly contradicts the intent to provide consumers with the protections afforded by that state's laws, potentially violating consumer protection laws like the CLRA. The original text ensures California law governs the interpretation of the contract, while the modified text opens the door to interpretations under other, potentially less consumer-friendly, jurisdictions. Snippet 1 states in CIV 1770 (a) that unfair or deceptive acts or practices are unlawful, which this omission could enable, as it undermines the consumer's right to the protections of California law.

perturbation.original_text: Company and BUYER mutually agree to hold the other party's Confidential Information in strict confidence and not to disclose such Confidential Information to any third parties except after receiving prior consent by the disclosing party in writing. Company and BUYER shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information as each employs with respect to its own proprietary information of like importance or a greater degree if reasonable.
perturbation.changed_text: Company and BUYER mutually agree to hold the other party's Confidential Information in confidence and not to disclose such Confidential Information to any third parties. Company and BUYER shall use a reasonable degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'strict' and 'same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information as each employs with respect to its own proprietary information of like importance or a greater degree if reasonable' weakens the protection of confidential information. This creates a loophole for negligent disclosure, potentially violating California's trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text replaces 'strict confidence' and detailed care standards with a vague 'reasonable degree of care.' This diminishes the legal protection afforded to trade secrets under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). CUTSA requires a higher standard of care to prevent misappropriation, and this change weakens that standard.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the protection of confidential information by replacing 'strict confidence' and specific care standards with a vague 'reasonable degree of care'. The law explanation suggests this change diminishes the legal protection afforded to trade secrets under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which requires a higher standard of care. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code (CIV) 3426, which refers to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Although the snippet is brief, it confirms the existence of CUTSA within California law, supporting the claim that weakening confidentiality protections could contradict legal requirements for safeguarding trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be modified only by written amendment signed by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be modified by amendment.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for a *written* amendment signed by both parties allows for potential verbal or implied modifications, which are not legally enforceable in California. This creates ambiguity and can lead to disputes over the terms of the agreement, undermining consumer protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1698 generally requires that modifications to written contracts be in writing. By removing the 'written amendment signed by both parties' clause, the modified agreement is open to potential verbal modifications, which are not typically enforceable under California law. This weakens the contract's clarity and enforcement, undermining protections for both parties, but especially consumers.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1698. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Modification and Cancellation [1697 - 1701] ( Heading of Chapter 3 amended by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109. ) 1698. (a) A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing. (b) A contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement to the extent that the oral agreement is executed by the parties. (c) Unless the contract otherwise expressly provides, a contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement supported by new consideration. The statute of frauds (Section 1624) is required to be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (d) Nothing in this section precludes in an appropriate case the application of rules of law concerning estoppel, oral novation and substitution of a new agreement, rescission of a written contract by an oral agreement, waiver of a provision of a written contract, or oral independent collateral contracts. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109.)
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the requirement for a written amendment signed by both parties. California Civil Code Section 1698(a) states, "A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing." The original text aligns with this, while the changed text opens the door for oral modifications, which are less enforceable and can undermine the clarity of the agreement, as the Law Explanation notes. This directly contradicts the principle that written contracts should be modified in writing, potentially weakening the contract's legal standing.
